Sensory system used for hearing

| Auditory system | |
| --- | --- |
| Identifiers | |
| [FMA](/wiki/Foundational_Model_of_Anatomy "Foundational Model of Anatomy") | [7192](https://bioportal.bioontology.org/ontologies/FMA/?p=classes&conceptid=http%3A%2F%2Fpurl.org%2Fsig%2Font%2Ffma%2Ffma7192) |
| [Anatomical terminology](/wiki/Anatomical_terminology "Anatomical terminology") [[edit on Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q821413 "d:Q821413")] | |

How sounds make their way from the source to the brain

The **auditory system** is the [sensory system](/wiki/Sensory_system "Sensory system") for the sense of [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing"). It includes both the [sensory organs (the ears)](/wiki/Ear "Ear") and the auditory parts of the [sensory system](/wiki/Sensory_system "Sensory system").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid14714940-1)

## System overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: System overview")]

The [outer ear](/wiki/Outer_ear "Outer ear") funnels [sound](/wiki/Sound "Sound") vibrations to the [eardrum](/wiki/Eardrum "Eardrum"), increasing the sound pressure in the middle frequency range. The [middle-ear](/wiki/Middle_ear "Middle ear") [ossicles](/wiki/Ossicles "Ossicles") further amplify the vibration pressure roughly 20 times. The base of the [stapes](/wiki/Stapes "Stapes") couples vibrations into the [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea") via the [oval window](/wiki/Oval_window "Oval window"), which vibrates the [perilymph](/wiki/Perilymph "Perilymph") liquid (present throughout the [inner ear](/wiki/Inner_ear "Inner ear")) and causes the [round window](/wiki/Round_window "Round window") to bulb out as the oval window bulges in.[[1]](#cite_note-pmid14714940-1)

[Vestibular](/wiki/Vestibular_duct "Vestibular duct") and [tympanic ducts](/wiki/Tympanic_ducts "Tympanic ducts") are filled with perilymph, and the smaller [cochlear duct](/wiki/Cochlear_duct "Cochlear duct") between them is filled with [endolymph](/wiki/Endolymph "Endolymph"), a fluid with a very different ion concentration and voltage.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Vestibular duct perilymph vibrations bend [organ of Corti](/wiki/Organ_of_Corti "Organ of Corti") outer cells (4 lines) causing [prestin](/wiki/Prestin "Prestin") to be released in cell tips. This causes the cells to be chemically elongated and shrunk ([somatic motor](/wiki/General_somatic_efferent_fibers "General somatic efferent fibers")), and hair bundles to shift which, in turn, electrically affects the [basilar membrane](/wiki/Basilar_membrane "Basilar membrane")'s movement (hair-bundle motor). These motors (outer [hair cells](/wiki/Hair_cells "Hair cells")) amplify the traveling wave [amplitudes](/wiki/Amplitudes "Amplitudes") over 40-fold.[[5]](#cite_note-pmid10821263-5) The outer hair cells (OHC) are minimally innervated by [spiral ganglion](/wiki/Spiral_ganglion "Spiral ganglion") in slow (unmyelinated) reciprocal communicative bundles (30+ hairs per [nerve fiber](/wiki/Nerve_fiber "Nerve fiber")); this contrasts with inner hair cells (IHC) that have only afferent innervation (30+ nerve fibers per one hair) but are heavily connected. There are three to four times as many OHCs as IHCs.

The [basilar membrane](/wiki/Basilar_membrane "Basilar membrane") (BM) is a barrier between scalae, along the edge of which the IHCs and OHCs sit. Basilar membrane width and stiffness vary to control the frequencies best sensed by the IHC. At the cochlear base the BM is at its narrowest and most stiff (high-frequencies), while at the cochlear apex it is at its widest and least stiff (low-frequencies). The [tectorial membrane](/wiki/Tectorial_membrane "Tectorial membrane") (TM) helps facilitate cochlear amplification by stimulating OHC (direct) and IHC (via endolymph vibrations). TM width and stiffness parallels BM's and similarly aids in frequency differentiation.[[6]](#cite_note-pmid17496047-6)[[7]](#cite_note-pmid20329841-7)[[8]](#cite_note-pmid12782348-8)[[9]](#cite_note-pmid11087000-9)[[10]](#cite_note-pmid3043645-10)[[11]](#cite_note-pmid24865766-11)

The [superior olivary complex](/wiki/Superior_olivary_complex "Superior olivary complex") (SOC), in the [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons"), is the first convergence of the left and right cochlear pulses. SOC has 14 described nuclei; their abbreviation are used here (see [Superior olivary complex](/wiki/Superior_olivary_complex "Superior olivary complex") for their full names). MSO determines the angle the sound came from by measuring time differences in left and right info. LSO normalizes sound levels between the ears; it uses the sound intensities to help determine sound angle. LSO innervates the IHC. VNTB innervate OHC. MNTB inhibit LSO via glycine. LNTB are glycine-immune, used for fast signalling. DPO are high-frequency and tonotopical. DLPO are low-frequency and tonotopical. VLPO have the same function as DPO, but act in a different area. PVO, CPO, RPO, VMPO, ALPO and SPON (inhibited by glycine) are various signalling and inhibiting nuclei.[[12]](#cite_note-pmid11071718-12)[[13]](#cite_note-pmid11071719-13)[[14]](#cite_note-pmid11071722-14)[[15]](#cite_note-pmid10066281-15)

The trapezoid body is where most of the cochlear nucleus (CN) fibers decussate (cross left to right and vice versa); this cross aids in sound localization.[[16]](#cite_note-pmid11520638-16) The CN breaks into ventral (VCN) and dorsal (DCN) regions. The VCN has three nuclei.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Bushy cells transmit timing info, their shape averages timing jitters. Stellate (chopper) cells encode sound spectra (peaks and valleys) by spatial neural firing rates based on auditory input strength (rather than frequency). Octopus cells have close to the best temporal precision while firing, they decode the auditory timing code. The DCN has 2 nuclei. DCN also receives info from VCN. Fusiform cells integrate information to determine spectral cues to locations (for example, whether a sound originated from in front or behind). Cochlear nerve fibers (30,000+) each have a most sensitive frequency and respond over a wide range of levels.[[17]](#cite_note-pmid10320216-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Simplified, nerve fibers' signals are transported by bushy cells to the binaural areas in the [olivary complex](/wiki/Olivary_complex "Olivary complex"), while signal peaks and valleys are noted by stellate cells, and signal timing is extracted by octopus cells. The lateral lemniscus has three nuclei: dorsal nuclei respond best to bilateral input and have complexity tuned responses; intermediate nuclei have broad tuning responses; and ventral nuclei have broad and moderately complex tuning curves. Ventral nuclei of lateral lemniscus help the inferior colliculus (IC) decode amplitude modulated sounds by giving both phasic and tonic responses (short and long notes, respectively). IC receives inputs not shown, including:

* visual (pretectal area: moves eyes to sound. superior colliculus: orientation and behavior toward objects, as well as eye movements (saccade)) areas,
* [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons") (superior cerebellar peduncle: [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") to [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum") connection/hear sound and learn behavioral response),
* spinal cord (periaqueductal grey: hear sound and instinctually move), and
* thalamus.

The above are what implicate IC in the 'startle response' and ocular reflexes. Beyond multi-sensory integration IC responds to specific amplitude modulation frequencies, allowing for the detection of pitch. IC also determines time differences in binaural hearing.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The medial geniculate nucleus divides into:

* ventral (relay and relay-inhibitory cells: frequency, intensity, and binaural info topographically relayed),
* dorsal (broad and complex tuned nuclei: connection to somatosensory info), and
* medial (broad, complex, and narrow tuned nuclei: relay intensity and sound duration).

The auditory cortex (AC) brings sound into awareness/perception. AC identifies sounds (sound-name recognition) and also identifies the sound's origin location. AC is a topographical frequency map with bundles reacting to different harmonies, timing and pitch. Right-hand-side AC is more sensitive to tonality, left-hand-side AC is more sensitive to minute sequential differences in sound.[[20]](#cite_note-pmid12481131-20)[[21]](#cite_note-pmid11305897-21)

Rostromedial and ventrolateral prefrontal cortices are involved in activation during tonal space and storing short-term memories, respectively.[[22]](#cite_note-pmid10570492-22) The Heschl's gyrus/transverse temporal gyrus includes Wernicke's area and functionality, it is heavily involved in emotion-sound, emotion-facial-expression, and sound-memory processes. The entorhinal cortex is the part of the 'hippocampus system' that aids and stores visual and auditory memories.[[23]](#cite_note-pmid17675110-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The supramarginal gyrus (SMG) aids in language comprehension and is responsible for compassionate responses. SMG links sounds to words with the angular gyrus and aids in word choice. SMG integrates tactile, visual, and auditory info.[[25]](#cite_note-pmid7600087-25)[[26]](#cite_note-pmid19232583-26)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Anatomy_of_the_Human_Ear.svg/250px-Anatomy_of_the_Human_Ear.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Anatomy_of_the_Human_Ear.svg)

Anatomy of the human ear (The length of the auditory canal is exaggerated in this image.).

Brown is [outer ear](/wiki/Outer_ear "Outer ear").

Red is [middle ear](/wiki/Middle_ear "Middle ear").

Purple is [inner ear](/wiki/Inner_ear "Inner ear").

### Outer ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Outer ear")]

Main article: [Outer ear](/wiki/Outer_ear "Outer ear")

The folds of cartilage surrounding the ear canal are called the [auricle](/wiki/Pinna_(anatomy) "Pinna (anatomy)"). Sound waves are reflected and attenuated when they hit the auricle, and these changes provide additional information that will help the brain determine the sound direction.

The sound waves enter the [auditory canal](/wiki/Auditory_canal "Auditory canal"), a deceptively simple tube. The ear canal amplifies sounds that are between 3 and 12 [kHz](/wiki/KHz "KHz").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid14714940-1) The [tympanic membrane](/wiki/Eardrum "Eardrum"), at the far end of the ear canal marks the beginning of the [middle ear](/wiki/Middle_ear "Middle ear").

### Middle ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Middle ear")]

Main article: [Middle ear](/wiki/Middle_ear "Middle ear")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Slide1ghe.JPG/250px-Slide1ghe.JPG)](/wiki/File:Slide1ghe.JPG)

Auditory ossicles from a deep dissection of the tympanic cavity

Sound waves travel through the ear canal and hit the tympanic membrane, or [eardrum](/wiki/Eardrum "Eardrum"). This wave information travels across the air-filled middle ear cavity via a series of delicate bones: the [malleus](/wiki/Malleus "Malleus") (hammer), [incus](/wiki/Incus "Incus") (anvil) and [stapes](/wiki/Stapes "Stapes") (stirrup). These [ossicles](/wiki/Ossicles "Ossicles") act as a lever, converting the lower-pressure eardrum sound vibrations into higher-pressure sound vibrations at another, smaller membrane called the [oval window](/wiki/Oval_window "Oval window") or vestibular window. The [manubrium](/wiki/Ear#Middle_ear "Ear") (handle) of the malleus articulates with the tympanic membrane, while the footplate (base) of the stapes articulates with the oval window. Higher pressure is necessary at the oval window than at the tympanic membrane because the inner ear beyond the oval window contains liquid rather than air. The [stapedius reflex](/wiki/Stapedius_reflex "Stapedius reflex") of the middle ear muscles helps protect the inner ear from damage by reducing the transmission of sound energy when the [stapedius muscle](/wiki/Stapedius_muscle "Stapedius muscle") is activated in response to sound. The middle ear still contains the sound information in wave form; it is converted to nerve impulses in the [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea").

### Inner ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Inner ear")]

| Cochlea | |
| --- | --- |
| Diagrammatic longitudinal section of the cochlea. The [cochlear duct](/wiki/Cochlear_duct "Cochlear duct"), or *scala media*, is labeled as *ductus cochlearis* at right. | |
| Identifiers | |
| [FMA](/wiki/Foundational_Model_of_Anatomy "Foundational Model of Anatomy") | [7192](https://bioportal.bioontology.org/ontologies/FMA/?p=classes&conceptid=http%3A%2F%2Fpurl.org%2Fsig%2Font%2Ffma%2Ffma7192) |
| [Anatomical terminology](/wiki/Anatomical_terminology "Anatomical terminology") [[edit on Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q821413 "d:Q821413")] | |

Main article: [Inner ear](/wiki/Inner_ear "Inner ear")

The inner ear consists of the [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea") and several non-auditory structures. The cochlea has three fluid-filled sections (i.e. the *scala media, scala tympani and scala vestibuli)*, and supports a fluid wave driven by pressure across the [basilar membrane](/wiki/Basilar_membrane "Basilar membrane") separating two of the sections. Strikingly, one section, called the cochlear duct or *[scala media](/wiki/Scala_media "Scala media"),* contains [endolymph](/wiki/Endolymph "Endolymph"). The organ of Corti is located in this duct on the basilar membrane, and transforms mechanical waves to electric signals in neurons. The other two sections are known as the *[scala tympani](/wiki/Scala_tympani "Scala tympani")* and the *[scala vestibuli](/wiki/Scala_vestibuli "Scala vestibuli").* These are located within the bony labyrinth, which is filled with fluid called [perilymph](/wiki/Perilymph "Perilymph"), similar in composition to cerebrospinal fluid. The chemical difference between the fluids [endolymph](/wiki/Endolymph "Endolymph") and [perilymph](/wiki/Perilymph "Perilymph") fluids is important for the function of the inner ear due to electrical potential differences between potassium and calcium ions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The plan view of the human cochlea (typical of all [mammalian](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") and most [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate")) shows where specific frequencies occur along its length. The frequency is an approximately exponential function of the length of the cochlea within the [Organ of Corti](/wiki/Organ_of_Corti "Organ of Corti"). In some species, such as bats and dolphins, the relationship is expanded in specific areas to support their active sonar capability.

#### Organ of Corti

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Organ of Corti")]

Main article: [Organ of Corti](/wiki/Organ_of_Corti "Organ of Corti")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Cochlea-crosssection.svg/250px-Cochlea-crosssection.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cochlea-crosssection.svg)

The [organ of Corti](/wiki/Organ_of_Corti "Organ of Corti") located at the *[scala media](/wiki/Scala_media "Scala media")*

The organ of Corti forms a ribbon of sensory epithelium which runs lengthwise down the cochlea's entire *scala media*. Its hair cells transform the fluid waves into nerve signals. The journey of countless nerves begins with this first step; from here, further processing leads to a panoply of auditory reactions and sensations.

#### Hair cell

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Hair cell")]

Main article: [Hair cell](/wiki/Hair_cell "Hair cell")

Hair cells are columnar cells, each with a "hair bundle" of 100–200 specialized [stereocilia](/wiki/Stereocilia "Stereocilia") at the top, for which they are named. There are two types of hair cells specific to the auditory system; *inner* and *outer* *hair* *cells*. Inner hair cells are the mechanoreceptors for hearing: they transduce the vibration of sound into electrical activity in [nerve fibers](/wiki/Nerve_fiber "Nerve fiber"), which is transmitted to the brain. Outer hair cells are a motor structure. Sound energy causes changes in the shape of these cells, which serves to amplify sound vibrations in a frequency specific manner. Lightly resting atop the longest [cilia](/wiki/Cilia "Cilia") of the inner hair cells is the [tectorial membrane](/wiki/Tectorial_membrane_(cochlea) "Tectorial membrane (cochlea)"), which moves back and forth with each cycle of sound, tilting the cilia, which is what elicits the hair cells' electrical responses.

Inner hair cells, like the [photoreceptor cells](/wiki/Photoreceptor_cell "Photoreceptor cell") of the eye, show a [graded response](/wiki/Postsynaptic_potential "Postsynaptic potential"), instead of the [spikes](/wiki/Action_potential "Action potential") typical of other neurons. These graded potentials are not bound by the "all or none" properties of an action potential.

At this point, one may ask how such a wiggle of a hair bundle triggers a difference in membrane potential. The current model is that cilia are attached to one another by "[tip links](/wiki/Stereocilia_(inner_ear)#Design_and_constellation "Stereocilia (inner ear)")", structures which link the tips of one cilium to another. Stretching and compressing, the tip links may open an ion channel and produce the receptor potential in the hair cell. Recently it has been shown that [cadherin-23 CDH23](/wiki/CDH23 "CDH23") and [protocadherin-15 PCDH15](/wiki/PCDH15 "PCDH15") are the adhesion molecules associated with these tip links.[[27]](#cite_note-Lelli_Kazmierczak_2010-27) It is thought that a [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium") driven motor causes a shortening of these links to regenerate tensions. This regeneration of tension allows for apprehension of prolonged auditory stimulation.[[28]](#cite_note-Peng_2011-28)

#### Neurons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Neurons")]

Main article: [Hair cell neural connection](/wiki/Hair_cell#Neural_connection "Hair cell")

Afferent neurons innervate cochlear inner hair cells, at synapses where the neurotransmitter [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate") communicates signals from the hair cells to the dendrites of the primary auditory neurons.

There are far fewer inner hair cells in the cochlea than afferent nerve fibers – many auditory nerve fibers innervate each hair cell. The neural dendrites belong to neurons of the [auditory nerve](/wiki/Auditory_nerve "Auditory nerve"), which in turn joins the [vestibular nerve](/wiki/Vestibular_nerve "Vestibular nerve") to form the [vestibulocochlear nerve](/wiki/Vestibulocochlear_nerve "Vestibulocochlear nerve"), or [cranial nerve](/wiki/Cranial_nerve "Cranial nerve") number VIII.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
The region of the basilar membrane supplying the inputs to a particular afferent nerve fibre can be considered to be its [receptive field](/wiki/Receptive_field "Receptive field").

Efferent projections from the brain to the cochlea also play a role in the perception of sound, although this is not well understood. Efferent synapses occur on outer hair cells and on afferent (towards the brain) dendrites under inner hair cells

## Neuronal structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Neuronal structure")]

### Cochlear nucleus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cochlear nucleus")]

Main article: [Cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Cochlear_nucleus "Cochlear nucleus")

The [cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Cochlear_nucleus "Cochlear nucleus") is the first site of the neuronal processing of the newly converted "digital" data from the inner ear (see also [binaural fusion](/wiki/Binaural_fusion "Binaural fusion")). In mammals, this region is anatomically and physiologically split into two regions, the [dorsal cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Dorsal_cochlear_nucleus "Dorsal cochlear nucleus") (DCN), and [ventral cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Ventral_cochlear_nucleus "Ventral cochlear nucleus") (VCN). The VCN is further divided by the nerve root into the posteroventral cochlear nucleus (PVCN) and the anteroventral cochlear nucleus (AVCN).[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Trapezoid body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Trapezoid body")]

Main article: [Trapezoid body](/wiki/Trapezoid_body "Trapezoid body")

The [trapezoid body](/wiki/Trapezoid_body "Trapezoid body") is a bundle of [decussating](/wiki/Decussation "Decussation") fibers in the ventral pons that carry information used for binaural computations in the brainstem. Some of these [axons](/wiki/Axon "Axon") come from the [cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Cochlear_nucleus "Cochlear nucleus") and [cross over](/wiki/Decussation "Decussation") to the other side before traveling on to the [superior olivary nucleus.](/wiki/Superior_olivary_complex "Superior olivary complex") This is believed to help with [localization of sound](/wiki/Sound_localization "Sound localization").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Superior olivary complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Superior olivary complex")]

Main article: [Superior olivary complex](/wiki/Superior_olivary_complex "Superior olivary complex")

The [superior olivary complex](/wiki/Superior_olivary_complex "Superior olivary complex") is located in the [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons"), and receives projections predominantly from the ventral cochlear nucleus, although the dorsal cochlear nucleus projects there as well, via the ventral acoustic stria. Within the [superior olivary complex](/wiki/Superior_olivary_complex "Superior olivary complex") lies the lateral superior olive (LSO) and the medial superior olive (MSO). The former is important in detecting interaural level differences while the latter is important in distinguishing [interaural time difference](/wiki/Interaural_time_difference "Interaural time difference").[[14]](#cite_note-pmid11071722-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Lateral_lemniscus.PNG/250px-Lateral_lemniscus.PNG)](/wiki/File:Lateral_lemniscus.PNG)

**Lateral lemniscus** in red, as it connects the [cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Cochlear_nucleus "Cochlear nucleus"), [superior olivary nucleus](/wiki/Superior_olivary_nucleus "Superior olivary nucleus") and the [inferior colliculus](/wiki/Inferior_colliculus "Inferior colliculus"), seen from behind

### Lateral lemniscus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Lateral lemniscus")]

Main article: [Lateral lemniscus](/wiki/Lateral_lemniscus "Lateral lemniscus")

The lateral lemniscus is a tract of [axons](/wiki/Axon "Axon") in the [brainstem](/wiki/Brainstem "Brainstem") that carries information about sound from the [cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Cochlear_nucleus "Cochlear nucleus") to various brainstem nuclei and ultimately the contralateral [inferior colliculus](/wiki/Inferior_colliculus "Inferior colliculus") of the [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain").

### Inferior colliculi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Inferior colliculi")]

Main article: [Inferior colliculus](/wiki/Inferior_colliculus "Inferior colliculus")

The inferior colliculi (IC) are located just below the visual processing centers known as the [superior colliculi](/wiki/Superior_colliculus "Superior colliculus"). The central nucleus of the IC is a nearly obligatory relay in the ascending auditory system, and most likely acts to integrate information (specifically regarding sound source localization from the [superior olivary complex](/wiki/Superior_olivary_complex "Superior olivary complex")[[13]](#cite_note-pmid11071719-13) and [dorsal cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Dorsal_cochlear_nucleus "Dorsal cochlear nucleus")) before sending it to the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") and [cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid14714940-1) The inferior colliculus also receives descending inputs from the [auditory cortex](/wiki/Auditory_cortex "Auditory cortex") and auditory [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") (or [medial geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Medial_geniculate_nucleus "Medial geniculate nucleus")).[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Medial geniculate nucleus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Medial geniculate nucleus")]

Main article: [Medial geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Medial_geniculate_nucleus "Medial geniculate nucleus")

The [medial geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Medial_geniculate_nucleus "Medial geniculate nucleus") is part of the thalamic relay system.

### Primary auditory cortex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Primary auditory cortex")]

Main article: [Primary auditory cortex](/wiki/Primary_auditory_cortex "Primary auditory cortex")

The [primary auditory cortex](/wiki/Primary_auditory_cortex "Primary auditory cortex") is the first region of [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") to receive auditory input.

Perception of sound is associated with the left posterior [superior temporal gyrus](/wiki/Superior_temporal_gyrus "Superior temporal gyrus") (STG). The superior temporal gyrus contains several important structures of the brain, including [Brodmann areas](/wiki/Brodmann_areas "Brodmann areas") 41 and 42, marking the location of the [primary auditory cortex](/wiki/Primary_auditory_cortex "Primary auditory cortex"), the cortical region responsible for the sensation of basic characteristics of sound such as pitch and rhythm. We know from research in nonhuman primates that the primary auditory cortex can probably be divided further into functionally differentiable subregions.[[33]](#cite_note-pmid8578069-33)[[34]](#cite_note-pmid9575378-34)[[35]](#cite_note-pmid10322185-35)[[36]](#cite_note-pmid11050211-36)
[[37]](#cite_note-pmid11745645-37)[[38]](#cite_note-pmid12536133-38)[[39]](#cite_note-pmid11303104-39)
The neurons of the primary auditory cortex can be considered to have [receptive fields](/wiki/Receptive_field "Receptive field") covering a range of [auditory frequencies](/wiki/Audio_frequency "Audio frequency") and have selective responses to harmonic pitches.[[40]](#cite_note-pmid24381544-40) Neurons integrating information from the two ears have receptive fields covering a particular region of auditory space.

The primary auditory cortex is surrounded by secondary auditory cortex, and interconnects with it. These secondary areas interconnect with further processing areas in the [superior temporal gyrus](/wiki/Superior_temporal_gyrus "Superior temporal gyrus"), in the dorsal bank of the [superior temporal sulcus](/wiki/Superior_temporal_sulcus "Superior temporal sulcus"), and in the [frontal lobe](/wiki/Frontal_lobe "Frontal lobe"). In humans, connections of these regions with the [middle temporal gyrus](/wiki/Middle_temporal_gyrus "Middle temporal gyrus") are probably important for [speech perception](/wiki/Speech_perception "Speech perception"). The frontotemporal system underlying auditory perception allows us to distinguish sounds as speech, music, or noise.

### The auditory ventral and dorsal streams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The auditory ventral and dorsal streams")]

Main article: [Language processing in the brain](/wiki/Language_processing_in_the_brain "Language processing in the brain")

Further information: [Two-streams hypothesis § Two auditory systems](/wiki/Two-streams_hypothesis#Two_auditory_systems "Two-streams hypothesis")

From the primary auditory cortex emerge two separate pathways: the auditory ventral stream and auditory dorsal stream.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The auditory ventral stream includes the anterior superior temporal gyrus, anterior superior temporal sulcus, middle temporal gyrus and temporal pole. Neurons in these areas are responsible for sound recognition, and extraction of meaning from sentences. The auditory dorsal stream includes the posterior superior temporal gyrus and sulcus, [inferior parietal lobule](/wiki/Inferior_parietal_lobule "Inferior parietal lobule") and intra-parietal sulcus. Both pathways project in humans to the inferior frontal gyrus. The most established role of the auditory dorsal stream in primates is sound localization. In humans, the auditory dorsal stream in the left hemisphere is also responsible for speech repetition and articulation, phonological long-term encoding of word names, and verbal working memory.

### Ascending Auditory Pathway: Coding Mechanisms and Experience-Dependent Plasticity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Ascending Auditory Pathway: Coding Mechanisms and Experience-Dependent Plasticity")]

The ascending auditory pathway transmits acoustic information from the cochlea to auditory cortex through a series of subcortical nuclei, including the cochlear nucleus, superior olivary complex, inferior colliculus, and medial geniculate body. Throughout these stages, several organizational principles—particularly tonotopy, the ordered mapping of frequency—are preserved from the cochlea to cortex.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The pathway supports both sound localization and sound identification, with the latter being critical for speech and language.

#### Rate Coding in the Auditory Nerve

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Rate Coding in the Auditory Nerve")]

The basilar membrane converts frequency information into a place code, but intensity is also encoded through firing rate. Increased sound levels produce larger basilar membrane displacements, leading to greater hair-cell depolarization and higher firing rates in auditory nerve fibers.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Auditory nerve fibers vary in spontaneous firing rates. High-spontaneous-rate fibers respond at very low thresholds but saturate quickly, whereas medium- and low-spontaneous-rate fibers encode higher intensities.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
This “division of labor” enables fine-grained representation of intensity across a wide dynamic range, crucial for speech perception.

#### Limitations of Place Coding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Limitations of Place Coding")]

Pure place coding cannot account for all aspects of pitch perception. As harmonic frequencies increase, cochlear filters become too broad to resolve adjacent harmonics.[[45]](#cite_note-45) This limitation becomes more pronounced at higher sound intensities, motivating the need for temporal coding mechanisms.

#### Temporal Coding and Phase Locking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Temporal Coding and Phase Locking")]

In temporal (time-based) coding, auditory nerve fibers synchronize their firing to specific phases of the acoustic waveform, a process known as phase locking.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Although neurons do not fire on every cycle, they fire preferentially near waveform peaks, allowing representation of periodicity even when place cues are ambiguous.

Phase locking underlies the frequency-following response (FFR), a scalp-recorded electrophysiological signal that mirrors the timing, pitch, and harmonic structure of the stimulus.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Speech-evoked FFRs reproduce stimulus periodicity with such fidelity that the response waveform can generate intelligible speech when played back.

#### Subcortical Response Timing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Subcortical Response Timing")]

Auditory processing in the brainstem occurs on the scale of milliseconds. Recordings from humans and nonhuman primates show activation in the inferior colliculus within 5–10 ms of stimulus onset, with thalamic and cortical responses following shortly after.[[48]](#cite_note-48)
This rapid temporal precision is essential for speech onset detection, phoneme discrimination, and sound localization.

#### Experience-Dependent Plasticity in the Auditory Brainstem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Experience-Dependent Plasticity in the Auditory Brainstem")]

Although cortical areas exhibit the most dramatic plasticity, evidence shows that the auditory brainstem is also shaped by experience via descending corticofugal pathways.

Musical Training

Musicians show:

larger FFR amplitudes

faster onset responses

enhanced representation of F0 and harmonics

superior pitch tracking of dynamic contours (e.g., Mandarin tones)

These enhancements appear to reflect strengthened cortico-brainstem interactions.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Short-Term Training

Even brief (≈1 hour) phonetic discrimination training increases FFR harmonic power, whereas passive listening does not.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
This demonstrates rapid, learning-dependent modulation of subcortical encoding.

#### Descending (Corticofugal) Pathways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Descending (Corticofugal) Pathways")]

The descending auditory pathway projects from auditory cortex to the inferior colliculus, superior olivary complex, and cochlear nucleus. These projections adjust subcortical gain, sharpen tuning, and enhance behaviorally relevant features.[[51]](#cite_note-51)
Thus, while sensory information travels upward, experience and attention travel downward, dynamically shaping early auditory coding.

#### Relevance to Speech and Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Relevance to Speech and Language")]

The ascending auditory pathway supports language by encoding:

formant structure and harmonic content

rapid temporal cues essential for stop consonants and prosody

pitch contours relevant for tone languages

stable representations shaped by linguistic experience

Tonotopy is preserved into auditory cortex, where neurons in the superior temporal gyrus and sulcus integrate this information into language-specific categories.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auditory_system&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

Proper function of the auditory system is required to able to sense, process, localizalizing a sound's origin in space and understand sound from the surroundings, even in noise background.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Difficulty in sensing, processing and understanding sound input has the potential to adversely impact an individual's ability to communicate, learn and effectively complete routine tasks on a daily basis.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In children, early diagnosis and treatment of impaired auditory system function is an important factor in ensuring that key social, academic and speech/language developmental milestones are met.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Impairment of the auditory system can include any of the following:

* [Auditory brainstem response](/wiki/Auditory_brainstem_response "Auditory brainstem response") and ABR audiometry test for newborn hearing
* [Auditory processing disorder](/wiki/Auditory_processing_disorder "Auditory processing disorder")
* [Hyperacusis](/wiki/Hyperacusis "Hyperacusis")
* [Diplacusis](/wiki/Diplacusis "Diplacusis")
* [Tinnitus](/wiki/Tinnitus "Tinnitus")
* [Endaural phenomena](/wiki/Endaural_phenomena "Endaural phenomena")
